Potato___Early_blight.summary_en: Early blight (Alternaria solani) — a fungal disease that shows up as brown target-like rings on older potato leaves.
Potato___Early_blight.symptoms: Small dark brown spots with concentric rings ('target spots') on lower, older leaves; surrounding tissue yellows and leaves may drop.
organic: Pick off and destroy infected lower leaves early.
Space plants and remove weeds so leaves dry quickly after rain.
Spray a neem-based or copper-based fungicide as a protectant.
Mulch the soil to stop spores splashing up from the ground.
chemical: Apply a protectant fungicide such as mancozeb or chlorothalonil, following the product label rate and interval.
prevention: Rotate potato with non-host crops for 2-3 years.
Use certified disease-free seed tubers and resistant varieties where available.
Avoid overhead irrigation; water at the base.
Keep plants well-fed with balanced nitrogen so they resist infection.
Potato___Early_blight.expert_note: Fungicide names here are general classes — always follow the label rate and pre-harvest interval, and check with your local (upazila) agriculture officer.

Potato___Late_blight.summary_en: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans) — a fast-moving disease that can destroy a potato field in days during cool, wet weather.
Potato___Late_blight.symptoms: Water-soaked dark green to brown blotches on leaves and stems, often with a white fuzzy mould on the underside in humid weather; spreads very fast.
organic: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately — do not compost them.
Improve drainage and airflow to keep foliage dry.
Spray copper-based fungicide protectively before symptoms worsen.
chemical: Use a protectant fungicide (mancozeb or chlorothalonil) before infection; switch to a systemic blight fungicide if the disease is already active, per label.
prevention: Plant certified seed and resistant varieties.
Destroy volunteer potatoes and nearby cull piles.
Avoid overhead watering; scout fields closely in cool, humid spells.
Hill soil over tubers so spores can't reach them.
Potato___Late_blight.expert_note: Late blight moves fast — if several plants are affected, act the same day and consult your local agriculture officer about the right fungicide.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.summary_en: Bacterial spot (Xanthomonas) — a bacterial disease spread by splashing water and handling wet plants.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small dark, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown with a yellow halo; scabby raised spots on fruit.
organic: Remove and destroy infected leaves and debris.
Spray a copper-based product regularly as a protectant.
Avoid touching or harvesting plants while they are wet.
chemical: Copper combined with mancozeb is the usual recommendation; follow the label — note bacteria are not cured by ordinary fungicides.
prevention: Start with certified disease-free seed and transplants.
Rotate away from tomato and pepper for 2-3 years.
Use drip irrigation instead of overhead watering.
Disinfect stakes and tools between uses.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.expert_note: Copper slows bacterial spread but won't cure it — prevention and sanitation matter most. Check with your local agriculture officer.

Tomato___Early_blight.summary_en: Early blight (Alternaria) on tomato — brown target-ring spots that start on the lowest leaves.
Tomato___Early_blight.symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings on older leaves, yellowing around them, and progressive leaf drop from the bottom up.
organic: Remove infected lower leaves and mulch the soil surface.
Stake plants and space them for good airflow.
Apply neem or copper fungicide as a protectant.
chemical: Protectant fungicides such as chlorothalonil or mancozeb, applied per label at first symptoms.
prevention: Rotate crops and remove crop residue after harvest.
Water at the base, keeping foliage dry.
Choose resistant varieties where available.
Tomato___Early_blight.expert_note: Follow the label rate and pre-harvest interval; consult your local agriculture officer for the right product.

Tomato___Late_blight.summary_en: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans) on tomato — the same aggressive disease as potato late blight.
Tomato___Late_blight.symptoms: Large greasy grey-green to brown blotches on leaves and stems, sometimes with white mould underneath; fruit develops firm brown patches.
organic: Pull and destroy infected plants right away.
Improve spacing and airflow; avoid wetting the leaves.
Spray copper fungicide protectively.
chemical: Protectant mancozeb or chlorothalonil before infection; a systemic late-blight fungicide if already active, per label.
prevention: Use resistant varieties and healthy transplants.
Avoid overhead irrigation and crowded planting.
Scout closely during cool, wet weather.
Tomato___Late_blight.expert_note: Act the same day you see it — late blight spreads fast. Consult your local agriculture officer.

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.summary_en: Leaf mould (Passalora fulva) — a fungal disease that thrives in humid, poorly ventilated conditions.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.symptoms: Pale yellow patches on the upper leaf surface with an olive-green to grey velvety mould on the underside; leaves yellow and drop.
organic: Increase ventilation and reduce humidity around plants.
Remove and destroy affected leaves.
Apply copper fungicide as a protectant.
chemical: Chlorothalonil or mancozeb protectant sprays per label.
prevention: Space and prune plants for airflow; avoid leaf wetness.
Use resistant varieties, especially under cover.
Water early in the day so leaves dry fast.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.expert_note: Managing humidity and airflow usually does more than spraying. Consult your local agriculture officer if it persists.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.summary_en: Septoria leaf spot (Septoria lycopersici) — a fungal disease that peppers the lower leaves with many small spots.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.symptoms: Numerous small circular spots with dark brown margins and grey centres on lower leaves; heavy spotting causes yellowing and defoliation.
organic: Remove infected lower leaves and destroy crop debris.
Mulch to stop soil splash; keep foliage dry.
Apply copper fungicide protectively.
chemical: Chlorothalonil or mancozeb, applied per label at first sign.
prevention: Rotate crops and practise good field sanitation.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
Stake and space plants for airflow.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.expert_note: Sanitation and rotation are key. Follow the fungicide label and ask your local agriculture officer.

Tomato___Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.summary_en: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV) — a virus carried by whiteflies. There is no chemical cure for the virus itself, so you control the whitefly.
Tomato___Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.symptoms: Upward curling and cupping of leaves, yellow leaf edges, stunted bushy growth, and heavy flower/fruit drop.
organic: Remove and destroy infected plants to cut the source.
Use yellow sticky traps and neem oil to reduce whiteflies.
Lay reflective/silver mulch to repel whiteflies.
Protect young plants with fine insect netting.
chemical: There is no spray that cures the virus — manage the whitefly vector with an approved insecticide, rotating modes of action, per label.
prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant varieties where available.
Control whiteflies early and remove infected plants promptly.
Keep the field and borders free of weed hosts.
Tomato___Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.expert_note: Because it's a virus, focus on whitefly control and resistant varieties, not on 'curing' the plant. Consult your local agriculture officer.

Corn___Common_rust.summary_en: Common rust (Puccinia sorghi) on maize — usually manageable and often does not need spraying.
Corn___Common_rust.symptoms: Small cinnamon to reddish-brown powdery pustules scattered on both surfaces of the leaves; heavy infection yellows the leaves.
organic: Plant resistant hybrids — the most effective step.
Remove heavily infected volunteer plants.
Ensure balanced nutrition so plants tolerate infection.
chemical: A foliar fungicide (triazole or strobilurin class) is only worthwhile if infection is early and severe on a susceptible crop, per label.
prevention: Choose resistant hybrids and plant on time.
Avoid very dense planting that traps humidity.
Corn___Common_rust.expert_note: Common rust rarely justifies spraying on a healthy crop. Ask your local agriculture officer before applying fungicide.

Corn___Northern_Leaf_Blight.summary_en: Northern leaf blight (Exserohilum turcicum) on maize — a fungal disease that can cut yield when it strikes early.
Corn___Northern_Leaf_Blight.symptoms: Long, cigar-shaped grey-green to tan lesions running along the leaf; lesions merge and dry out large areas of leaf.
organic: Grow resistant hybrids.
Rotate crops and plough under old maize residue where the fungus survives.
Remove volunteer maize plants.
chemical: A foliar fungicide applied at first lesions (around tasseling) protects yield on susceptible crops, per label.
prevention: Use resistant hybrids and rotate away from maize.
Manage crop residue by tillage or removal.
Corn___Northern_Leaf_Blight.expert_note: Timing matters — early protection pays off, late spraying rarely does. Consult your local agriculture officer.

Pepper___Bacterial_spot.summary_en: Bacterial spot (Xanthomonas) on pepper — a bacterial disease spread by water splash and handling.
Pepper___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small dark, water-soaked spots on leaves that become brown and angular; leaves yellow and drop, and fruit gets scabby spots.
organic: Remove and destroy infected leaves and debris.
Spray copper-based product as a protectant.
Don't work among plants when they are wet.
chemical: Copper plus mancozeb is the usual protectant combination; bacteria are not cured by ordinary fungicides, so follow the label and rely on prevention.
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed and transplants.
Rotate away from pepper and tomato for 2-3 years.
Switch to drip irrigation and disinfect tools.
Pepper___Bacterial_spot.expert_note: Prevention and clean seed matter more than any spray. Consult your local agriculture officer.

Rice___Bacterial_leaf_blight.summary_en: Bacterial leaf blight (Xanthomonas oryzae) — a serious bacterial disease of rice that spreads in warm, wet, flooded conditions.
Rice___Bacterial_leaf_blight.symptoms: Water-soaked yellow streaks along the leaf edges that spread and turn the leaf greyish-white and dry; young plants can wilt ('kresek').
organic: Drain the field periodically to reduce spread where possible.
Remove and destroy severely infected plants and weed hosts on bunds.
Avoid excessive nitrogen, which makes plants more susceptible.
chemical: There is no reliable chemical cure; copper-based sprays give limited protection. Focus on resistant varieties and nutrient management, per local advice.
prevention: Grow resistant varieties suited to your area.
Use clean, certified seed and treat seed before sowing.
Apply balanced fertiliser and avoid over-application of nitrogen.
Keep bunds weed-free and manage water carefully.
Rice___Bacterial_leaf_blight.expert_note: Bacterial blight is managed mainly through resistant varieties and good nutrient/water management, not sprays. Consult your local agriculture officer.

Rice___Blast.summary_en: Rice blast (Magnaporthe oryzae) — one of the most damaging rice diseases worldwide, affecting leaves, nodes and the neck of the panicle.
Rice___Blast.symptoms: Diamond- or spindle-shaped spots with grey centres and brown margins on leaves; dark lesions on the neck below the panicle that can cause the grain head to break or stay empty.
organic: Remove infected stubble and weed hosts after harvest.
Avoid heavy nitrogen and split the nitrogen dose instead of applying all at once.
Keep a steady water level; avoid drought stress that worsens blast.
chemical: Preventive fungicides (tricyclazole or a strobilurin, per label) are used at the leaf stage and again before heading on susceptible varieties, following local recommendations.
prevention: Grow blast-resistant varieties.
Use certified, treated seed.
Manage nitrogen carefully and maintain even water.
Do not plant too densely.
Rice___Blast.expert_note: Neck blast around heading is the most damaging stage — time any preventive spray before heading and consult your local agriculture officer.

Rice___Brown_spot.summary_en: Brown spot (Bipolaris oryzae) — a fungal disease often linked to poor or unbalanced soil nutrition.
Rice___Brown_spot.symptoms: Many small, oval brown spots scattered across the leaves (and on grains); heavy spotting on nutrient-poor soils reduces grain filling.
organic: Correct the underlying soil nutrition, especially potassium and micronutrients.
Use well-decomposed organic matter to improve soil health.
Remove infected debris after harvest.
chemical: Seed treatment and a protectant fungicide (e.g. mancozeb) can help on badly affected crops, per label and local advice.
prevention: Test and correct soil fertility before planting.
Use certified, treated seed and resistant varieties.
Avoid water stress during the season.
Rice___Brown_spot.expert_note: Brown spot is often a 'hungry soil' disease — fixing nutrition usually helps more than spraying. Consult your local agriculture officer.

Rice___Tungro.summary_en: Rice tungro — a virus disease spread by green leafhoppers; there is no cure for the virus, so you manage the insect and use resistant varieties.
Rice___Tungro.symptoms: Yellow to orange discolouration of leaves starting from the tip, stunted growth, and reduced tillering; patches of affected plants in the field.
organic: Remove and destroy infected plants and volunteer/ratoon rice that host the virus.
Encourage natural enemies of leafhoppers and use light traps to monitor them.
Synchronise planting with neighbours and avoid staggered planting that keeps hoppers active.
chemical: No spray cures the virus — manage the green leafhopper vector with an approved insecticide only when monitoring shows high numbers, per label.
prevention: Grow tungro-resistant varieties where available.
Plant early and in synchrony with the community.
Control leafhoppers and remove infected plants promptly.
Rice___Tungro.expert_note: Because tungro is a virus, focus on resistant varieties and leafhopper control rather than curing the plant. Consult your local agriculture officer.
